name: Thousand-Year-Old Eggs
recipeIngredient: 2 cups Each ashes of pine wood,
Ashes of charcoal and ashes from fireplace
recipeInstructions:
These are often called thousand-year eggs, even though the preserving process lasts only 100 days.
They may be purchased individually in Oriental markets.
Combine tea, salt, ashes, and lime. Using about 1/2 cup per egg, coat each egg completely with this clay-like mixture.
Line a large crock with garden soil and carefully lay coated eggs on top.
Cover with more soil and place crock in a cool, dark place. Allow to cure for 100 days.
To remove the coating, scrape eggs and rinse thoroughly under running water.
Crack lightly and remove shells. The white of the egg will appear grayish, translucent color and have a gelatinous texture. The yolk, when sliced, will be grayish-green in color.
Serve cut into wedges with sweet pickled scallions or any sweet pickled vegetable and sauce made from vinegar, soy sauce, rice wine, and minced ginger root.
Available at garden stores and nurseries.
